Business owner comes back from seminar all jazzed up and he's got five pages of ideas. He's got some CDs under his arm and he's ready to go. And the staff has their little meeting. We've seen this before. Pretty much if we just go into slow-mo and wait it out, it'll all wear off and things will go back to normal in about two weeks. And if it doesn't, then here's our scheme, here's our plan of how we handle this, which one is extortion. It's well okay doc, we can start doing these concern calls at the end of every day, but that means we won't be able to bill insurance on time. Which is one step away from, okay we'll do that, but we'll have to cut your baby's head off and eat it. So the staff gets it away, there's outright laziness. And ultimately, because it's a lot easier just to throw money at advertising. Sure. It's a lot easier to do that than it is to think this all the way through and put a referral system in place. So there's a lot of sloth. And last, there's the only legitimate, not really knowing what to do and not knowing how to do it. Do you think there's some fear involved though too? I mean some folks are just like... Now I think that's the ego stuff. You always really think it's ego. Yeah, I think it's about how I'll be perceived and I'll be perceived negatively. Or needy? Well, yeah, so unsuccessful, in need, pushy, salesy. There's a whole range of negative emotions. But they're all ego driven. And ironically, if you actually do a good job, you do anything significant in people's lives, you deliver goods, then it's also actually an act of anti-empathy. Absolutely. I mean it's not really being helpful. And so you're really letting your ego get in the way, not just of your money, but actually of your service to community, etc. But yeah, I think people are very much inhibited by what they think people will think of what it is that they do. Going back to the first point they made, I've heard you say in the past about marketing that people think, well, because I'm the best at what I do, I should just get customers, clients, or patients coming in. It's the entitlement. It's the same thing with referrals. Yeah, see the entitlement list is because I've got a PhD and my competitor doesn't, because I've got a cooler office, because I do better work, because I've been here longer, because there's a long list of reasons for entitlement. Business owners in reality often are not much different than the union mentality of a worker, which is the longer I'm at this, the easier it should get to get customers, and the more money I should get. Right. I've been in this community for 37 years, and he opened his office three days ago. I'm entitled to, well, and people don't say, they don't always enunciate it as entitlement, but that's exactly what it is. Sure, well, they're not even consciously aware of it, probably. So now we know why business owners don't ask for referrals. I guess the question is to the entrepreneur and the business owner watching this right now is, well, how come customers just don't automatically refer if I do a good job, if I'm a nice guy, if I have five kids, so on and so forth? Well, so there's two big broad answers. Okay. One is almost nothing happens automatically except bad things. Almost nothing happens automatically except bad things. So a car's life extended to 100,000 miles, or I just saw, and I don't think they made a commercial out of it like Subway did with Jared the morons, but some woman was on the news for Toyota just turned its millionth mile. Millionth. Yeah, she's got some kind of route. She drives 800 miles a week or something. There are a million miles on this Toyota pickup. Well, that didn't happen automatically. She got the oil change. She took care of this truck. The trucks collapse at planned obsolescence of 25,000 miles right after the warranty, and everything starts going bad. That happens automatically. You don't have to do a thing. Just drive it. Okay. So bad things happen automatically. Good things never do. The second big reason is it's not their job.
